title: Cohort Retention Analysis
business_definition: Cohort retention tracks what percentage of users acquired in a given period are still active N periods later. It isolates product quality from acquisition volume. Healthy retention curves flatten at a non-zero level.
when_to_use: are newer users retaining as well as older users
diagnostic_questions: Are newer cohorts retaining worse than older cohorts — indicating a product regression?
Does retention flatten out or go to zero — indicating whether there is a loyal core user base?
At which interval does the biggest retention drop occur — day 1, week 1, month 1?
Do specific segments (e.g. activated vs non-activated users) show very different retention curves?

causal_relationships.symptom: retention at day 30 declining across cohorts
causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT cohort_month, interval_month, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS retained, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / FIRST_VALUE(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)) OVER (PARTITION BY cohort_month ORDER BY interval_month) AS retention_rate FROM (SELECT u.user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', u.created_at) AS cohort_month, DATEDIFF('month', u.created_at, a.activity_date) AS interval_month FROM users u JOIN user_activity a USING (user_id)) t GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2

causal_relationships.symptom: steep drop-off at day 1 or week 1
causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', signup_date) AS cohort, COUNT(*) AS signups, COUNT(CASE WHEN last_active >= signup_date + INTERVAL '7 days' THEN 1 END) AS retained_7d, COUNT(CASE WHEN last_active >= signup_date + INTERVAL '30 days' THEN 1 END) AS retained_30d FROM users GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

inflation_causes.cause: Passive sessions (e.g. background API calls) counted as activity — overstates true retention
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT activity_type, COUNT(*) FROM user_activity GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC

deflation_causes.cause: Users who changed email/account not linked to original cohort
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM users WHERE merged_into_user_id IS NOT NULL

anti_patterns: Do not use rolling averages for cohort analysis — each cohort must be tracked independently
Do not define 'active' as any login — use meaningful engagement events

sql_assets.cohort_retention_grid: SELECT cohort_month, interval_month, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users FROM (SELECT u.user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', u.created_at) AS cohort_month, DATEDIFF('month', u.created_at, a.activity_date) AS interval_month FROM users u JOIN user_activity a USING (user_id) WHERE a.activity_date >= u.created_at) t GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2
